meta.note: v9.3 (2026-06-21): meta.version and meta.schema_version are now tracked separately. version is the content/release counter (bumps on any meaningful edit: new services, retagged smart_tags, new modules, etc). schema_version is the STRUCTURAL compatibility counter — it only bumps when the shape of the JSON changes in a way that would make old code misread the file (renamed/removed keys, new required fields, changed nesting). schema_version 1.0 represents the v9.1-and-later shape (intake_modules consolidation + params field on intake_chain steps was the last breaking structural change). v9.2 was pure qr.html engine fixes with no JSON shape change. v9.3 added checkout_state_override to the shared effects definition — additive and optional, so backward-compatible with schema_version 1.0 (old code ignoring an unrecognized field still reads the file correctly) — does not bump schema_version. See CHANGELOG_v9.1.md / v9.2.md / v9.3.md for full per-version detail; this note only tracks the version/schema_version split itself.

estimate_disclaimers.flat_fee: This price is final for the described labor unless the scope changes on site. Materials (if any) are extra and will be confirmed before work begins.
estimate_disclaimers.hourly: This price is final for the described labor unless the scope changes on site. Materials (if any) are extra and will be confirmed before work begins.
estimate_disclaimers.project_based: All estimates are preliminary and non‑binding. Your final, binding price will be confirmed after our technician inspects the job site and you approve the exact scope and material selections. You are not committed until you accept the final quote.

confidence_escalation._note: Applied live by the pricing engine when a detected/tapped smart_tag carries complexity_override. Worst escalation among all active tags wins — deltas do not stack additively across multiple tags. This lets an unnamed/dynamic_services job (e.g. 'Other Wall Mounting: neon sign on brick') tighten its own confidence bar mid-session without needing a hand-authored service for every possible material/condition combo. max_followup_questions_absolute caps total questions regardless of escalation — even a genuinely complex job shouldn't face more than 6 questions, or 'minimal friction' stops meaning anything.

diagnostic_governance._rule: checkout_state == 'diagnostic' IF AND ONLY IF confidence_strategy._variability_tier == 'diagnostic'. variability_tier is the single source of truth for this — checkout_state should be treated as DERIVED from it, not independently authored. This was previously an unenforced coincidence (verified true for all services at time of writing, but nothing prevented a future edit from breaking it).
diagnostic_governance._consuming_code_should: derive checkout_state from variability_tier when both are absent/ambiguous, and treat a stored checkout_state that contradicts variability_tier as a data error to flag, not silently trust.

pax_wardrobe_formula._note: Moved from base64-obfuscated PAX_COEFF_B64 constant in qr.html. Was: pricePerHour=85, startFee=0, coefficients={cabinet:1.3, standardDoor:0.6, slidingDoor:0.9, insideItem:0.35}. Edit here, not in application code.

database_summation.ui_message: Add your specific items from the catalog below.

diagnostic.ui_message: This issue requires on-site diagnostics to provide a precise quote.

standard_flat_rate.ui_message: Standardized labor estimate. Materials are extra and estimated below.

ui_config._note: All user-facing label strings. JS reads these; never hardcode them.

adlib._note: Natural-English phrase templates for adlib sentence. JS reads tag.ui_phrase first; falls back to group template.

negation_library._note: Word lists for NLP negation detection. JS reads these; no inline regex needed.

action_conjugations._note: SSOT for verb-form matching AND ad-lib infinitive phrasing. Keyed by service_types key where applicable, so it can never drift from service_types[*].verb_natural. Built to fix the bug where 'window fixed' was detected as action='service' (generic fallback) instead of action='Repair', because the old detectAction() regex only matched bare infinitives ('fix') and not conjugated forms ('fixed'). Consumers should build ONE matching regex per action from all_forms+synonyms (word-boundary, case-insensitive), and use infinitive_for_adlib for 'I need to ___' sentence construction.

default_fallback.note: Generic 'Other' job – uses repair base rate

contextual_overrides._note: Typing mount a tv/flatscreen resolves to the dedicated service at $60 rather than the generic mounting fallback at $75

contextual_overrides.note: Mounting a shelf, mirror, frame, or picture -- the generic 'Frames | Shelves | Misc' bucket -- rather than the even more generic mounting fallback. v9.5 fix, found after the conjugation fix let mount legitimately outscore shelf/mirror's own specific keywords on raw weight, exposing that mount had no override for these (unlike its existing tv override).

objects._note: v9.5 FIX: added real, plain single-word verb synonyms after a natural-phrasing test corpus confirmed every rigid, multi-word synonym ('hang on wall', 'attach to wall', etc.) missed common, natural real phrasing entirely -- 'hang a picture on the wall', 'attach a mirror to the wall', and 'fix a bracket to the wall' all previously scored ZERO on this keyword and fell through to drywall_repair_formula instead, the wrong formula for a mounting job. Kept the original rigid multi-word synonyms too (harmless, still correct when they DO match) and added the plain verbs matching this catalog's normal, working convention elsewhere.

contextual_overrides.note: Replacing the wax ring/seal specifically, not the whole toilet or its internal flapper/fill valve -- v9.5 fix, found via an external analysis's real test corpus.

Installing a whole new toilet, not repairing an existing one's internals -- v9.5 fix, found via natural-phrasing test corpus: 'install a new toilet' previously fell through to the generic default_service_sku (a $45 flapper/fill-valve repair) despite a real, correct toilet_install ($100) service existing.
Bidet attachment only, not full toilet install
Installing a new washer, not repairing an existing one
Setting up a new microwave, not repairing an existing one

Backing up the data on the drive, not replacing the physical drive itself -- v9.5 fix, found via an external analysis's real test corpus ('back up my hard drive' was incorrectly resolving to internal_hardware_replacement).

appliance_type._orphan_backlog_note: v9.1 audit: this module is fully authored (question + client_response + fee/minute bands) but not referenced by any current service intake_chain. Left as-is rather than force-wired into a loosely-matching service, since guessing intent risks introducing a worse bug than leaving it unused (see CHANGELOG_v9.1.md — two real pre-existing wiring bugs were found and fixed this version; speculative wiring of the remaining orphans was intentionally deferred). Candidate for a future service that explicitly needs it, or removal if confirmed genuinely dead.

Major – extensive damage or large area

Not sure – I’ll describe in notes

client_response.label: I already have the toilet – just install it

wall_type.question: What kind of wall are we drilling into?

waterproof_area.question: Is the repair in a wet area like a shower or tub surround?

weight.question: About how heavy is the item?

window_type.question: What style of window is it?

client_response.label: Not sure – describe in notes (may add time)

item_count_template.question: How many {item_noun} need service?
item_count_template._template_note: Parameterized count-question template (v9.1). Replaces 12 near-duplicate single-use 'how many X' modules (outlet_count, bulb_count, switch_count, window_count, tile_count, device_count, extender_count, screen_count, dmg_count, count, item_count, scope_count). A service's intake_chain step supplies params.item_noun (substituted into {item_noun} above) and params.client_response to set the item-specific fee/minute bands. This keeps every service's exact original pricing behavior while removing 11 duplicate module definitions from the SSOT. See CHANGELOG_v9.1.md.

washer_type.question: What type of washing machine is it?
washer_type._orphan_backlog_note: v9.5: this module became orphaned as a direct, expected side effect of fixing shower_head_replacement's mis-wiring (it was the only service referencing washer_type; correcting that service to use existing_type -- the genuinely correct module for a shower-head fitting question -- left this one with no real referrer). Its question ("What type of washing machine is it?") is genuinely specific and real, not speculative content -- the same reasoning as the original v9.1 orphan batch applies: left as-is rather than force-wired into a loosely-matching service, since a real future washer-repair service could legitimately need exactly this question. Candidate for that future service, or removal if confirmed genuinely dead.

computer_component.question: Which part of the computer needs work?

tech_issue_source.question: What is the source of the problem?

device_state.question: What’s the current state of the device?

wall_mount_items.question: What kind of items are we mounting on the wall?

software_install_type.question: What do you need installed?
software_install_type._note: v9.1: added to fix a pre-existing data bug found during the v9.1 audit — software_or_driver_install was incorrectly wired to the washing-machine type module (pre-rename key 'type'), a stale copy-paste that predates this migration. This module asks the actually-relevant question for this service. v9.5: fixed 'Not sure' option, which previously cost MORE ($10/+10min/skilled) than a known, specific request ($0/routine) -- charging a premium for the customer's own uncertainty before any technician has assessed the actual job makes no sense and discourages honest communication. Now $0/routine, matching the other not-yet-fully-specified options; the real cost (if any) gets determined on-site once the tech actually sees what's needed.

tile_condition.question: What's the condition of the tile?

parking_difficulty.question: Is street parking difficult at this address?

disposal_request.question: Do you have an old item that needs to be hauled away?

pets_present.question: Do you have pets that may be loose in the home?

client_response.label: No pets / pets will be secured

urgency.question: How soon do you need this done?

item_volume.question: How many items does this job involve?

mounting_height.question: How high up will this be mounted or installed?

client_response.label: Handle feels loose or doesn't catch
Not sure — just want it checked

existing_toilet_type.question: What type of toilet do you have?

area_sqft.question: About how large is the damaged area?

client_response.label: 2 sq ft (a small patch, about a sheet of paper)
10 sq ft (about a dinner-plate to basketball-sized hole)
25 sq ft (a few feet across, e.g. a section of hallway wall)
50 sq ft or more (a large wall section or multiple spots)

area_sqft._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes drywall_repair_formula's area_sqft input. Labels are deliberately formatted as 'NUMBER unit (description)' rather than plain descriptive text, since applyPricingFormula reads answers.area_sqft via parseInt(label) directly (confirmed: answers[moduleKey] is always the chosen label string, never a derived number) -- this format gives the customer real, concrete context while still parsing correctly to the exact leading integer. Distinct from the existing dmg_size module (a separate, valid, less-precise visual-size impression question, e.g. 'fits in your hand') -- dmg_size was confirmed to never drive any real fee/minutes (all effects are 0, only complexity_override), so this new module is the genuine, first real pricing input for square footage, not a duplicate.

texture_match.question: Does the surrounding wall have a textured finish (e.g. orange peel, popcorn) that needs to be matched?
texture_match._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes drywall_repair_formula's texture_match input. applyPricingFormula checks answers.texture_match === 'Yes' exactly (confirmed via direct read of the real consuming code), so the second answer's label is the literal string 'Yes', matching that exact check.

grout_repair.question: Does this job involve repairing or replacing grout?
grout_repair._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes tile_repair_formula's grout_repair input. applyPricingFormula checks answers.grout_repair === 'Yes' exactly (confirmed via direct read of the real consuming code).

water_damage.question: Is there any water damage or softness around the tile (not just the tile itself)?
water_damage._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes tile_repair_formula's water_damage input. applyPricingFormula checks answers.water_damage === 'Yes' exactly. Distinct from waterproof_area (an existing, separate, real question about whether the AREA is a wet zone like a shower surround) -- this asks whether DAMAGE has already occurred, a genuinely different real-world condition with its own real cost (potential subfloor/backer-board work), not a duplicate.

ceiling_height.question: Is this tile work on a high or vaulted ceiling?
ceiling_height._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes tile_repair_formula's ceiling_height input. applyPricingFormula checks answers.ceiling_height === 'High ceiling' exactly (the literal string, confirmed via direct read). Deliberately NOT reusing the existing, shared mounting_height module -- its real labels ('High (8-16 ft, ladder needed)', etc.) don't match this exact comparison string, and mounting_height is already depended on by 14 other real, height-variable services; changing its labels to fit this one formula risked regressing those services for no real benefit. A new, dedicated module avoids that risk entirely.

disposal.question: Do you need the old tile/debris hauled away?
disposal._note: v9.5: real, new module authored to close backlog item A — closes tile_repair_formula's disposal input. applyPricingFormula checks answers.disposal === 'Yes' exactly.

pax_cabinet_count.question: How many PAX cabinet frames need to be assembled?

client_response.label: 6 or more (specify exact count in notes)

pax_cabinet_count._note: v9.5: real, exact-count labels with an explicit effects.qty_value, NOT open-ended bands like '4+' -- confirmed via direct test that the original delegated design's parseInt(label) approach silently truncated '4+' to 4 AND '2-3' to 2 (a second, related flaw beyond the one already caught), undercounting every multi-unit real job. applyPricingFormula now reads qty_value directly, never infers a count from label text.

pax_hinge_count.question: How many hinged doors need to be installed?

client_response.label: 6 or more (specify exact count in notes)

pax_hinge_count._note: v9.5: same real fix as pax_cabinet_count -- exact-count labels with explicit qty_value.

pax_sliding_count.question: How many sliding doors need to be installed?

client_response.label: 5 or more (specify exact count in notes)

pax_sliding_count._note: v9.5: same real fix as pax_cabinet_count -- exact-count labels with explicit qty_value.

pax_interior_count.question: How many interior shelf/drawer units need to be installed?

client_response.label: 7 or more (specify exact count in notes)

pax_interior_count._note: v9.5: interior units realistically span a wider, less consequential range (0.35 hr/unit is the smallest real coefficient) -- kept as bands with an explicit qty_value using the band's conservative low-end-plus-context value, rather than single-number rows, since this question alone isn't worth the same one-row-per-number precision as cabinet/hinge/sliding (whose coefficients are 3-4x larger and where undercounting matters more).

workflow._note: Phase 0 (Orchestrator Overhaul). This node is the active rulebook for routing, intake-question selection, confidence gating, and pricing -- not passive documentation. Every entry path (Catalog browse, Other-tile tap, free-text SmartQuote) must produce a BookingContext and pass it through executeWorkflow(context, db), which walks these steps in order. See Phase 5.5's _archaeology_notes on individual rules below for the specific real bug each rule exists to prevent -- these were extracted from actual hardcoded logic found and fixed during the architecture-audit sessions, not invented speculatively.

steps._note: Resolves BookingContext -> a concrete service or dynamic_service entity. For entry='catalog' or 'other_tile', selectedServiceId/selectedGroupId already pin this down directly. For entry='free_text', this step delegates to intent_mappings.objects keyword/synonym matching (NOT a hardcoded keyword map -- see the architecture-audit finding that resolveGroupFromIntent's hardcoded rules and intent_mappings.objects were two parallel, partially-overlapping systems; this workflow makes intent_mappings.objects the single real source going forward).

fallback._warn: no entity resolved with sufficient confidence

steps._note: Computes a small set of NAMED, independently-auditable booleans -- NOT a single opaque chainIsQtyOnly flag. Archaeology finding: the legacy chainIsQtyOnly check was too coarse to correctly handle behavior.bypass_intake services, 3 of which (high_ceiling_bulb_replacement, shower_head_replacement, tub_spout_replacement) have ONE real, price-affecting, non-quantity question that must still be asked even though the service is otherwise flagged for minimal friction. A single boolean cannot represent that distinction; named, separate flags can.

produces.chain_is_pure_quantity: true only if every module in the resolved intake_chain is a recognized quantity-style module (item_count, count, hybrid_qty, global_quantity)
produces.chain_is_single_simple_question: true if the chain has exactly one module AND that module is item_count_template (a banded quantity question) -- distinct from chain_is_pure_quantity, since item_count_template is NOT in the legacy quantity-module set but is still simple enough to bypass extensive intake when paired with behavior.bypass_intake
produces.chain_has_real_non_quantity_question: true if the chain contains any module that is neither a quantity-style module nor item_count_template -- when true, intake bypass rules MUST still surface that question; bypassing it would silently drop a real, price-affecting answer
produces.all_visible_real_questions_answered: true once every currently-visible (per _isModVisible) non-quantity module has a real answer in BookingContext.answers

steps._note: Builds the final, real list of intake questions to ask: entity.intake_chain (the named modules) + global_rules.force_modules_by_variability[entity.confidence_strategy._variability_tier] (universal logistics/height questions, injected only when not already present) + then-branch targets unlocked by already-given answers. Archaeology finding: an earlier version of this logic (S._forceModules) was SET but never CONSUMED in the legacy curated-intake renderer -- confirmed and fixed mid-session; this step makes that consumption a mandatory, auditable part of the workflow rather than something that can silently regress again.

Archaeology finding (direct feedback during the session): the location chip/NLP-extracted location was being treated as cosmetic filler/notes despite carrying real, derivable intent (a 'front door' implies solid-core, a 'bedroom door' implies hollow-core; a 'toilet' near 'guest bedroom' is genuinely ambiguous and must NOT be guessed). This step pre-fills an intake answer ONLY when context.extractedLocation matches exactly one client_response[*].location_hints entry for a given module -- never when zero or multiple answers claim the same location, and never overriding an answer the user already gave.

Archaeology finding: the legacy curated-intake renderer used a hardcoded tagBonus (+5 skilled / +10 specialized, ADDED TO THE SCORE) instead of global_rules.confidence_escalation's real +15/+30 deltas (which RAISE THE BAR). These are mechanically different operations that were both firing in the same wrong direction -- confirmed via direct trace to make the Estimate button enable roughly 20 points too early for a real service (flatscreen_mounting_standard + #brick_wall: bar should rise 50->65, legacy code left it at 50 while also inflating the score). This step performs ONLY the bar-raising operation (confidence_escalation), and a separate, additive scoring step (base_confidence + module gains + NLP match confidence) computes the score independently -- the two must never be conflated into one number again.
See ui_template_matrix below -- this is the corrected version of Phase 3's example, using the named flags from compute_variability_flags instead of a single chainIsQtyOnly boolean.

Archaeology finding (post-session audit): 51 services have real services.*.required_materials and 35 have optional_materials, referencing real materials_catalog SKUs with real prices and a real markup_percent (confirmed uniformly 20% in global_rules.surcharges) -- NONE of which is read at runtime; every materials display reads the separate, often-stale default_estimates.materials.min/max instead (confirmed 3 real services, e.g. dishwasher_repair, where the legacy number and the catalog-derived number would genuinely differ). Resolution, NOT deletion (Chesterton's Fence: default_estimates.materials.note frequently carries real, business-meaningful customer-facing explanation -- e.g. dishwasher_repair's 'Exact part cost cannot be determined before diagnosis' -- that has no equivalent in the catalog data and must be preserved): when required_materials/optional_materials is non-empty, compute min = sum(required SKU prices * (1+markup)), max = min + sum(optional SKU prices * (1+markup)), and use this as the authoritative materials range -- but ALWAYS carry forward default_estimates.materials.note verbatim if present, regardless of which range won.

Invokes the SINGLE unified pricing function for every entry path -- no separate _computePrice. Archaeology finding: the legacy Catalog-browse path's _computePrice never included global_rules.surcharges.dispatch_fee at all, while the SmartQuote path's computeUnifiedQuote always did -- the exact same service could quote differently by the full dispatch fee ($45) depending on which UI entry point was tapped. Confirmed and fixed mid-session by adding the fee to _computePrice directly (a stopgap); this workflow's real fix is that _computePrice no longer exists as a separate function at all once Phase 6 lands -- every entry path reaches this exact step.

ui_template_matrix._note: Determines UI layout. Evaluates top-down, first match wins. Corrected from the roadmap's original example: uses the NAMED flags from compute_variability_flags instead of a single chainIsQtyOnly boolean, specifically so the 3 real bypass_intake services with one genuine non-quantity question (high_ceiling_bulb_replacement, shower_head_replacement, tub_spout_replacement) are NOT routed into a template with no room to ask that question -- confirmed via direct check this session that those exact 3 services have complexity_tier:'skilled', not 'routine', ruling out a naive tier-based rule too.

rules._note: Archaeology Audit finding (confirmed via direct read of prefillSmartQuoteFromService): requires_furniture_selection and the isOther condition (default_tags includes #adhoc or #manual_review) BOTH completely bypass the curated-card/self-quote matrix today, diverting to the legacy showIntakeQuestions flow entirely -- a categorically different UI (a furniture item-picker grid, not a question card). Must be checked FIRST, before any confidence/chain-shape logic, exactly matching the legacy code's own early-return structure. Confirmed via direct data check: only furniture_assembly_flat_pack currently triggers this (requires_furniture_selection); the isOther/#adhoc/#manual_review condition is real, checkable code with zero services currently triggering it -- dormant today, not removed, since a future service could set those tags.

Same legacy_flow bypass as requires_furniture_selection above, triggered instead by default_tags containing #adhoc or #manual_review. Confirmed dormant today (zero real services currently set either tag) but the condition is real, live code in getServiceProfile()/prefillSmartQuoteFromService -- kept as an explicit rule so a future service authored with these tags is correctly routed, not silently mishandled by the matrix's other rules.

Pure quantity-only chain (or empty chain) -- the original, always-correct self-quote case. v9.5: confirmed via a complete catalog sweep that chain_is_pure_quantity's own definition (see orch_compute_variability_flags) correctly encodes the real, full legacy logic -- unconditional qty modules (global_quantity/item_count/etc, no bypass_intake needed) AND item_count_template specifically (which DOES require bypass_intake, checked inside the flag itself, not here) -- so this rule's condition is correctly simple; no separate bypass_intake gate belongs at the matrix level.

Archaeology fix: item_count_template-only services explicitly flagged bypass_intake (cabinet_knob_or_pull_install, door_lock_or_handle_install, led_bulb_upgrade) -- genuinely simple, but NOT chain_is_pure_quantity under the legacy module-name check, which is exactly why they never qualified for self-quote before this session's fix.

v9.5 FIX (the real, final fix, found by a complete catalog sweep -- two earlier attempts at this fix were each incomplete in a different way, see CHANGELOG/BACKLOG for the full story): a genuinely real, valid combination existed with no matching rule -- chain_is_pure_quantity:false (correctly, since the service isn't bypass_intake-eligible) AND chain_has_real_non_quantity_question:false (correctly, since item_count_template alone is never a real, distinguishing question). Confirmed via a complete catalog sweep this affected 10 real services (dimmer_switch_install, gfci_outlet_replacement, etc) -- each has exactly one real question (a qty prompt) that deserves a normal curated_card, not the chip_grid fallback this gap was silently routing them to. Placed after the self_quote rules (which correctly take priority when they DO match) and before the confidence-based catch-all.

Archaeology fix: high_ceiling_bulb_replacement / shower_head_replacement / tub_spout_replacement. MUST NOT route to self_quote -- that template has no room to ask the one real question these services have (mounting_height / existing_type), and forcing them through would silently drop a real, price-affecting answer. force_confidence (90-95 in the real data) applies ONLY once every visible real question is answered -- never before, since these values are deliberately HIGHER than the service's own minimum_quote_confidence and would otherwise let the Estimate button enable before the real question is answered.

Archaeology fix (caught by running the orchestrator against real data, not just reading the matrix on paper): a directly-resolved, real entity with real questions and NO bypass_intake flag was falling through to the confidence_score<60 catch-all (chip_grid/force_dynamic_fallback) -- categorically wrong for e.g. toilet_install, a real named service whose intake chain simply hasn't been answered yet. The confidence_score<60 rule is reserved for the genuinely unresolved/low-confidence free-text case; this rule correctly intercepts the much more common 'real entity, real questions, not yet answered' case first.

intake_bypass_rules._note: Phase 3 (separate from ui_template_matrix's UI-layout decision): determines whether the action/type-selection step (legacy 'Step 2') can be skipped because the answer is already unambiguous. Archaeology finding: the legacy builder (prefillSmartQuoteFromOtherTile/sqOpenBuilderPreseeded) already correctly implements this via uncoveredTypes.length === 1 -- this node makes that real, working logic an explicit, auditable rule rather than inline code only discoverable by reading the builder function.

rules._note: Exactly one possible service_type remains uncovered for this group -- the action is unambiguous, asking the customer to confirm it would be exactly the kind of self-evident question this project has repeatedly found and removed (e.g. asking if a front door is solid-core).

fallback._reason: multiple possible types remain genuinely ambiguous -- must ask

fallback_routing_rules._note: Phase 3: determines where to route when resolve_entity (workflow step 1) cannot pin down a specific named service. Archaeology finding: this exact 3-tier fallback chain (group match -> synthesize Other tile via resolve_dynamic_service; category match only -> generic wizard; no match at all -> intent_mappings.default_fallback) already exists correctly in the legacy sqAnalyze/prefillSmartQuoteFromOtherTile code -- this node makes it an explicit, auditable rule set. Does NOT change behavior; documents and formalizes what the verified-correct legacy code already does.

rules._note: category + group both resolved with reasonable confidence -- the most common real fallback case (e.g. 'mount a projector screen' correctly landing in wall_mounting_tv_flatscreen).

Category known but no specific group match -- ask the customer directly rather than guess a group, exactly the discipline that fixed the original 'groups[0] -> Appliances' misrouting bug found earlier this session.

Genuinely unresolved. Archaeology finding (still open, tracked separately): intent_mappings.default_fallback.service_id references 'generic_handyman_service', which does not exist as a real catalog service, and the field is also never read by any legacy code even when it did resolve. This rule formalizes the INTENDED behavior; the underlying data/content gap (authoring that service, or removing the dangling reference) remains separate, real backlog.

fallback._reason: absolute final safety net -- never silently fail to resolve to SOME bookable path

resolution_thresholds._note: Archaeology Audit finding: these two confidence thresholds were hardcoded in TWO places each (the legacy sqAnalyze AND the Phase 1 collectBookingContext_freeText collector), never read from the SSOT. auto_select_named_service is the bar a free-text match's _matchConfidence must clear to auto-select a specific named service outright (skip straight to its curated card). route_to_group_other_tile is the lower bar for routing to a resolved GROUP's synthetic Other tile when no single named service is confident enough. Both now live here as the single, real source -- orch_resolve_entity and collectBookingContext_freeText both read from this node instead of each carrying their own duplicate literal.

invariants.rule: A ResolvedRoute must be a real, non-null object
invariants._note: The most basic possible guarantee -- if the Orchestrator somehow produces undefined/null, nothing downstream should ever see it.

invariants.rule: uiTemplate must be one of the engine's real, defined templates
invariants._note: Catches a broken or stale ui_template_matrix rule producing a typo'd or removed template name -- exactly the failure mode this was first built to catch (a deliberately broken matrix.fallback during Phase 4 development).

invariants.rule: If uiTemplate is legacy_flow, a real entity must be resolved
invariants._note: legacy_flow specifically hands off to showIntakeQuestions, which requires a real service object -- a furniture-selection or adhoc bypass with no entity has nothing for the legacy renderer to work with.

invariants.rule: self_quote and curated_card require entityType to not be 'fallback'
invariants._note: A question-asking template needs a real, resolved entity to ask questions ABOUT -- entityType:fallback means resolve_entity (workflow step 1) never found one.
A real quote can never bill a negative amount -- catches a sign error or a pricing formula gone wrong upstream.
An inverted range (min > max) is never meaningful and would display nonsensically to a customer.

invariants.rule: trace must be a real, non-empty array
invariants._note: Phase 7 observability (and the Historian replay mechanism) depend on every real route carrying its own execution history -- a route with no trace can never be debugged or replayed.
